summary:
The Storming of the Bastille (July 14, 1789 AD) was the defining symbolic moment of the [[event:107|French Revolution]], when a Parisian crowd stormed and dismantled the royal fortress-prison of the Bastille, releasing its seven prisoners, killing its governor, and demonstrating that the revolutionary tide could not be stopped by royal force. The Bastille was a medieval fortress that had served as a state prison, symbolizing royal despotism — arbitrary imprisonment without trial — in the popular imagination, though by 1789 it held only seven prisoners (none political). The attack was triggered by rumors that King Louis XVI was massing troops around Paris to dissolve the National Assembly; the revolutionary crowds, already inflamed by the dismissal of the popular finance minister Necker, surged to the Bastille seeking weapons to defend the National Assembly.

After negotiations between the crowd and the governor Bernard de Launay collapsed, the crowds stormed the outer courtyards; when de Launay surrendered, he was seized by the crowd and decapitated, his head paraded through Paris on a pike. The National Assembly, hearing of the fall, called the event the beginning of liberty. Louis XVI, informed the next morning, reportedly asked 'Is it a revolt?' and was told 'No, Sire, it is a revolution.' The Bastille was demolished stone by stone over the following months; its fall anniversary (July 14, Bastille Day) became and remains the French national holiday. As a piece of practical history, the event's significance lay less in its military or humanitarian impact (seven prisoners freed) than in its demonstration that the Parisian crowd could act as a revolutionary force, deterring royal counter-revolution and empowering the National Assembly to continue its constitutional work.
